schools.summary: Henry: Carmel's victory changed nothing at court; Ahab told Jezebel not to convince but to exasperate her, and Elijah's courage suddenly collapses.
Keil and Delitzsch: Jezebel's threat is impotent rage; she dares not touch a prophet the people love and the king now half-fears.
Gill: Ahab rehearsed the miracles to clear himself; the courage Elijah showed was of the Lord, not of himself.
Clarke: Ahab told Jezebel probably with no evil design, merely reporting the day's events.

debates.question: What was Ahab's motive in telling Jezebel all that Elijah had done?

sides.position: deliberate provocation, to exasperate her against Elijah
probably no evil design, a mere report of events

schools.summary: Clarke renders the oath at full weight: a self-curse measuring Jezebel's fury.
Keil and Delitzsch: an oath of impotent wrath; she dares not seize him for fear of the people.
Gill: whether from scorn or providence, the warning gave Elijah time to escape.

debates.question: Why did Jezebel warn Elijah instead of killing him?

sides.position: providence overruled her rage, or she meant a public example she was not yet ready to make
impotent wrath — she dared not touch him for fear of the people

themes: the word meant for death becomes life

schools.summary: Gill: the man who faced Ahab now fears one woman, proving his courage was of the Lord, not himself.
Ellicott: the sudden despondency is characteristic of an impulsive nature; even in Judah he felt unsafe.
Clarke: prudence overtaken by discouragement when Carmel failed to convert the court; he spared his servant the danger.

themes: courage of the Lord not of self

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: God comes to help the despairing; Ellicott: 'not better than my fathers' betrays a wounded hope of standing out beyond all before him.
Gill: the day's journey into the wilderness of Paran, where Israel wandered forty years, foreshadowing Elijah's forty days.
Clarke: the prayer of a weary man — 'I have lived long enough; I can do no more good.'
Clarke: anguish and fatigue induce sleep; God sends an angel with what he needs.
Gill: the lone juniper, one tree for one lonely man.
Ellicott: the 'angel' ministers unearthly food, auxiliary to the prophet, 'as the angel of the Agony to the Prophet of prophets.'

themes: God meets us at our lowest

schools.summary: Gill: the cake baked on hot stones after the eastern manner, perhaps heated by an angel's supernatural power.
Clarke: the whole provision seems supernaturally supplied.
Keil and Delitzsch identify a Bedouin bread-cake for a way beyond his strength; Ellicott hears a pathetic touch in a man who eats too little and sleeps again.
Gill: the angel comes a second time and touches him to wake him — God will not let his servant perish in his sleep.
Keil and Delitzsch: 'the journey is too great for thee' — the second meal is equipment for a road Elijah has not yet been told to take.

themes: God measures care by our need

schools.summary: Clarke: he fasted the same forty days as Moses at Horeb and Christ in the wilderness.
Gill: the single meal carried him the whole desert road.
Keil and Delitzsch: the pilgrimage to Horeb was Elijah's own intention; Ellicott: forty days exceed a 200-mile march, implying an interval of retreat and meditation.

debates.question: Do the forty days measure the journey itself, or a season of solitary retreat?

sides.position: an interval of retirement and meditation, since the distance is far too short for forty days
a pilgrimage Elijah intended, strengthened for the long desert road

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: 'the cave' is the cleft where Moses stood; the question means to lead Elijah to pour out his heart. Ellicott: the word came in a night vision.
Henry: a double reproof of both the fleeing and the fixing — 'Should such a man as I flee?'
Gill: the cave is shown still; it cannot be Moses' cleft, else Elijah need not go forth on the mount.
Clarke: the question probes his flight from Jezebel through unbelieving fears.

debates.question: Is 'What doest thou here, Elijah?' a reproof or a gentle drawing-out?

sides.position: a rebuke of both his flight and his hiding
no reproof, but meant to lead him to pour out his heart

schools.summary: Gill: a true account of a true zeal for God's glory that had stirred Jezebel's revenge.
Keil and Delitzsch hear carnal zeal and a tacit reproof of God; Ellicott hears impatient remonstrance, like Isaiah's and Jonah's.
Clarke: a very affecting fourfold picture of apostate Israel — covenant forsaken, altars thrown down, prophets slain, the last hunted.

debates.question: How should Elijah's complaint be judged?

sides.position: carnal zeal and a despairing, impatient remonstrance against God's long-suffering

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: a repetition of Sinai, Elijah as a second Moses; Ellicott: God is not in the power but in the still small voice — 'not by might, but by my Spirit.'
Gill (with the Targum): the wind, quake, and fire are angelic hosts and emblems of a wrath God could loose but withholds in mercy.
Clarke: the terrors are heralds of the gentle voice, teaching that God comes in mercy, not judgment; the cause of it all is supernatural.

themes: not by might but by my Spirit

schools.summary: Gill: the fire is an angelic host and an emblem of a wrath God could unleash but withholds, showing mercy in the next emblem.
Keil and Delitzsch: tempest, quake, and fire are signs of coming judgment; by withholding himself from them God teaches his meaning in the gentle rustling.
Gill: he veils his face in reverence and in shame under a sense of his unworthiness, like the seraphim and Moses.
Clarke: covering the face is a token of respect among the Asiatics, as Moses dared not look on God.
Keil and Delitzsch: the whisper revealed God as merciful, but the answer showed he does not leave guilt unpunished.
Gill: Elijah says the same as before, his grief not yet consoled.
Keil and Delitzsch: the reply is framed so he learns, to his shame, that the Lord's cause is far less desperate than he thought.
Henry: God answers the discouragement with a threefold provision and the comfort of the hidden faithful — 'the Lord knows them that are his.'
Gill: literally to retrace his road to Damascus, or figuratively to return to his old work of preaching.
Clarke: God routes him away from his enemies and readies judgment on the land and the house of Ahab.
Ellicott: 'return' is a double rebuke — delegate the vengeance, and know the seven thousand you never saw.

themes: judgment on the house of Ahab

schools.summary: Gill: Jehu was anointed by Elisha at Elijah's direction, which counts as Elijah's own act.
Ellicott: Elijah fulfilled only one part in person; the imperfect correspondence marks the narrative as genuine history.
Clarke: 'anoint' means the call sealed by the mantle; he refutes Jarchi's 'once in the covenant, always in the covenant.'

debates.question: Was Elisha literally anointed with oil, or is 'anoint' a figure for the call?

sides.position: the question is open; no anointing of a prophet with oil is recorded
'anoint' means only the call, sealed by the mantle
a real anointing by Elisha, or Elisha twice anointed

schools.summary: Gill: many literally slain in Israel by both Hazael and Jehu, the survivors of one falling to the other.
Clarke: Elisha used no sword; the three are ministers of vengeance, and the order of time is not to be pressed.
Ellicott: Elisha destroyed enmity, not enemies; the charge is framed in terms Elijah would grasp, by the weapons of peace.

debates.question: In what sense is Elisha said to 'slay'?

sides.position: not literally — the sword of prophecy; the order of time is not to be regarded
by the weapons of peace, framed in terms Elijah would understand
many literally slain by Hazael and Jehu

schools.summary: Gill: the seven thousand are reserved from perishing by the sword of Hazael or Jehu.
Clarke: seven is a number of perfection, many thousands; the common people worshipped in secret. He unfolds the idiom of kissing the idol.
Ellicott: 'I will leave' the faithful remnant, like the sealed of Ezekiel and Revelation, through all the vengeance.
Keil and Delitzsch: the cast cloak is the symbol of the prophetic call; Ellicott: a wealthy man's simple call, the mantle an act of adoption.
Henry: found ploughing, not idle; an honest calling is no bar to the heavenly one; the mantle is a powerful, effectual call in a day of power.
Gill: the long road from Horeb to Abelmeholah.
Clarke: twelve yoke mark a considerable estate left at real secular loss; the mantle is the prophet's peculiar hair garb.
Gill: he ran because his heart was touched by the Lord and his mind enlightened to read the sign of the mantle.
Keil and Delitzsch: the reply lays no constraint on him; Ellicott: a half-ironical rebuke of seeming hesitation, echoing Luke 9:61-62.
Clarke: Elisha did not confer with flesh and blood; his call is from God, to whom he is accountable.

debates.question: What does Elijah's 'what have I done to thee?' mean?

sides.position: a release — no constraint laid, go freely; the call is from God
a half-ironical rebuke testing seeming hesitation

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: he slaughters the oxen, burns the plough for fuel, feasts the people, and follows as servant; Ellicott: a feast of farewell, henceforth a servant who poured water on Elijah's hands.
Gill: he returned, took leave at his father's house, and came again to the field.
Clarke: burning the plough tokens abandoned secular life; the call honours filial affection, and godly parents did not hinder him.
